text: The illusion of multitasking competence is self-reinforcing. Researchers Iqbal and Bailey found that people who switch tasks frequently report feeling more productive even when objective output measures decline. The sensation of busyness — the constant movement between inputs — registers as effort and effort registers as progress. Cal Newport, in "Deep Work," termed this "pseudo-productivity": the use of visible activity as a proxy for genuine output. The most damaging aspect of chronic task-switching may not be the cognitive cost in any single session but the gradual erosion of a person's tolerance for the boredom that precedes genuine depth.
question: What does Cal Newport mean by "pseudo-productivity"?

options.text: A state of productivity achieved through short, frequent work intervals rather than long sessions
The tendency to overestimate one's output when working with digital tools
The use of visible activity as a proxy for genuine output
A productivity technique that prioritizes shallow tasks to build momentum for deep work
